observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to
maintain the subsidiary records for property, plant and equipment or the Property,
Plant and Equipment Ledger Card (PPELC) required in Section 114 of Chapter 7 of
New Government Accounting System Manual for Local Government Units and
provide depreciation to all properties of the Municipality, to fairly present these
accounts in the financial statements.

We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the General Services
Officer to maintain the subsidiary records for property, plant and equipment or the
property cards prescribed in the accounting manual and reconcile the physical count
report with the accounting records, study thoroughly and resolve the noted
discrepancies, if any.

We recommended that the Municipality observe strictly the guidelines set forth in
Paragraph 29 of IPSAS 1, of the Handbook of International Public Sector Accounting
Pronouncements, 2020 Edition, Volume I for the fair presentation of its financial
statements and for providing useful information to the users for accountability
purposes and decision making purposes.

We recommended that the Municipality observe strictly the provisions of the IRR of
RA 7160; Section 3.17 of LBC No. 131 dated January 8, 2021 of the DBM; Section 3.7
of LBC No. 134, dated January 20, 2021 of DBM and Section 3.7 of LBC No. 130 dated
November 19, 2020 of the DBM:

Hence, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a. Municipal General Services Officer comply with the posting requirement in the
procurement of goods and infrastructures of the Municipality under the
competitive bidding;
b. Municipal Accountant account funds transfers in accordance with the guidelines
provided by the granting agency particularly LBCs issued by the DBM;
c. Municipal Treasurer return to the BTr the undisbursed/unexpended fund transfer
from national government agencies as provided in the guidelines on the
utilization of the transferred fund; and
d. Municipal Accountant submit all the necessary supporting documents pertaining
to the funds transferred to the Municipality for the audit period CY 2021.

We recommended that the Municipality always adhere to the provisions of Sections
4(3) and 4(8) of P.D. No. 1445 that trust fund be utilized for the specific purpose it was
intended and exercise sound fiscal administration in the local government operations
provided not contravening to existing laws and regulations.

We further recommended that concerned local government officials observe strictly
Section 86, also of P.D. No. 1445 that before entering into contract funds shall be
made available for the purpose.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and other concerned municipal officials
to ensure effective utilization of the 20 per cent Development Fund which is primarily
intended to fund projects that shall contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-
economic development and environmental management outcomes. Delays in the
completion of development projects definitely had an adverse impact to the
community development or realization of development objectives.

We further recommended that the development programs/projects/activities
contained in the Municipality’s LDIP be also those programs/projects/activities in its
Annual Investment Program that shall be the ones to be budgeted and implemented
pursuant to Sections 106 and 305(i) of RA No. 7160 and Article 410 of Act IRR.

We recommended that the Municipality observe strictly the provisions of Republic
Act No. 10121; accounting guidelines of Section 121 of Chapter 7 of New Government
Accounting System Manual for LGUs and rules and regulations of COA Circular
No. 2012-002:

a. Prioritize disaster response and early rehabilitation and recovery when the area is
declared under the state of calamity;
b. Prepare the annual LDRRMFIP according to the LDRRMP and as prescribed in the
aforementioned COA Circular;
c. Prepare the Report on Sources and Utilization of LDRRMF also, as prescribed in
the COA Circular No. 2012-002;
d. Transfer the unexpended/unobligated balance of Quick Response Fund and
LDRRMF – Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses for CY 2021 totaling
P522,438.00 and maintain the prescribed subsidiary ledgers for transfers of the
agency’s unutilized LDRRMF to the special trust fund by year of transfer; and
e. Support expensed inventory with RIS upon issuance to the beneficiaries.

We recommended that the Municipality fully implement the approved Municipal SWM
Plan covering the period CY 2017 to 2027 in compliance with declared policy of the
State to adopt a systematic, comprehensive and ecological solid waste management
program and knowing that the LGUs are primarily responsible for the implementation
and enforcement of the provisions of the Ecological Solid Waste Management
(ESWM) Act.

We recommended that the Local School Board strictly comply with the provisions of
Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2017 of the DepEd, DBM and DILG that the Annual and
Supplemental Budget are presented and approved in adherence to the said
guidelines.

We further recommended that the copy of SIP submitted for audit include the
certification of acceptance and approval issued by the Schools Division
Superintendent to show that the document does contain strategic prioritization
policies in the allocation of SEF to the schools.

We recommended that the Municipality stringently observe guidelines provided in
Sections C.1 (5) and C.3 (1) of JMC No. 2013-01 of the PCW, DILG, the DBM, and the
NEDA, to effectively plan and implement GAD PPAs of the LGU and that five percent
GAD budget shall endeavor to influence the remaining 95 per cent of the LGU budget
toward gender-responsiveness.

We recommended the Municipality submit the necessary supporting documents of
those transactions were deficiencies are noted and detailed in the Annex G.

Also, we recommended that Municipality ensure that succeeding disbursements are
completely documented specially to those pertaining to PPAs which post-monitoring is
essential to determine if the goals are attained by the Municipality.

Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the following:
a. The Municipal Treasurer conduct annually the physical count of all Property, Plant and Equipment and reconcile Report on Physical Count of Property Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) with the accounting records to fully identify the discrepancies
b. Complete all the fields required in the prescribed Reports on Physical Count and the necessary remarks.
c. The Accounting Department submit copies of PPE Ledger Cards and Schedule of PPE to the audit team for CY 2021 onwards. Have those records reconciled with the General Service Office (GSO) and document the results to be the bases of adjustments, if any. Likewise, ensure that the Subsidiary Ledgers/Schedule of PPE tally with the balances in the Financial Statement; and
d. The Municipal Engineer assist the General Services Office and the Municipal Accounting Office in the conduct of the physical count and reconciliation of PPEs particularly to their Infrastructure Assets and Buildings and Other Structures accounts. Ensure that all PPEs in those groups of accounts are accounted and verified.
observations.managementAction:
The noted discrepancies are brought by lack of records relative to whereabouts or location of the properties and equipment, others are due to typographical errors of unit cost in the Physical Count Schedules and others were submitted but still included in the list of PPEs with discrepancies.
Relative to the PPE Ledger card, the accounting prepared and submitted ledgers to COA with the same format as other GL accounts, but as recommended, the Accounting office is currently working on the proper format for the PPE Ledger cards, while the Treasury Office has already prepared the Property cards for Machineries and Equipment, Transportation Equipment and Furniture and Fixtures in accordance with Government Accounting Manual.
As they checked, the unidentified balances as of December 31, 2021 has a total of P16,635,655.40 comprising PPEs from General and Special Education Fund. Relative to this, the agency will continue to exert effort to identify and reconcile these items to fairly present the PPE accounts in The Municipality’s Financial Statements.
The School Buildings amounting to P1,994,095.41 mentioned above is part of the prior period balances that has no record if already fully depreciated hence no application of depreciation was made. For the office equipment, this is mainly comprised of the PPE funded by the Bayanihan Fund, which we overlooked that has a separate depreciation schedule. In connection with this, we will do necessary adjustments for prior year depreciation and fairly present the net book value of the said equipment in the financial statements of 2022.

observations.recommendation:
Based on the foregoing observations, we recommended that the Municipal
Mayor require the following:

a. The Municipal Treasurer conduct annually the physical count of all Property, Plant
and Equipment and reconcile RPCPPE with the accounting records to fully identify
the discrepancies noted. Complete all the fields required in the prescribed Reports
on Physical Count and the necessary remarks. With regard to disposal, complete the
necessary documents and perform the subsequent procedures in accordance with
the Circular. Likewise, ensure that all PPEs are fully supported with the PPE Cards;
b. The Accounting Department submit copies of PPE Ledger Cards and Lapsing
Schedule of PPE to the audit team for CY 2021 onwards. Have those records
reconciled with the GSO and document the results to be the bases of adjustments, if
any. Likewise, ensure that the Subsidiary Ledgers/Lapsing Schedule of PPE tally
with the balances in the Financial Statement.; and
c. The Municipal Engineer assist the General Services Office and the Municipal
Accounting Office in the conduct of the physical count and reconciliation of PPEs
particularly to their Infrastructure Assets and Buildings and Other Structures
accounts. Ensure that all PPEs in those groups of accounts are accounted and
verified.

Based on the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the
following:

a. The Inventory Committee headed by the Municipal Treasurer’s Office conduct the
physical count of its Local Road Network and prepare the RPCLRN. Make use of
the template provided in Annex C of COA Circular No. 2015-008 or Appendix 63 of
the Government Accounting Manual for LGUs and complete the necessary
information in every field. Submit the accomplished RPCLRN to the Accounting
Office for reconciliation with the books. Also prepare the Road Network Property
Card for each Local Road Network;
b. The Municipal Engineer provide cost segregation of every road networks owned by
the Municipality per road component for the prior and succeeding years. Submit the
road networks duly segregated per road component to the Municipal Accountant and
the Municipal Treasurer. Likewise, provide technical assistance to the inventory
committee in the conduct of physical count of its Local Road Networks; and
c. The Municipal Accountant provide the necessary Road Network Ledger Cards and
have it reconciled with the balance in the Financial Statements. As to the identified
Road Network projects, coordinate with the Municipal Engineer to provide individual
cost per road component to be the basis in the computation of depreciation
expenses.

We recommended that the Municipality conduct the periodic physical count of
inventories pursuant to Section 194 of the Government Accounting Manual for Local
Government Units to vouch for the physical existence of the year-end balance of the
reported inventories. Also, prepare the Report of the Physical Count of Inventories
(RPCI) and submit the report not later than January 31 of each year.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant apply consistently the Modified Accrual
Basis of accounting to Real Property Tax and Special Education Tax Receivables and
their deferred accounts.

We further recommended that the accounting guidelines in the reconciliation
of Statement of Comparison of Budget and Actual Amounts to the Statement of
Financial Performance be followed accordingly, particularly in the presentation of
reconciling items such as basis differences, timing differences and entity differences.

We recommended that the Municipality strictly comply with the prescribed
accounting method in the Government Accounting Manual for Local Government
Units for Trust Fund to show clearly and fairly in the financial statements of the
Municipality the incurred transactions under its Trust Fund.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant present in the financial
statements of special account - public market the cost of depreciation of the facility
and the book value of the structure to provide adequate information as to assets,
liabilities and equity and be able to determine whether the local economic enterprise
has become financially self-sustaining establishment.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant continue to reconcile the
municipal records with the province’s books of accounts to establish the correct
balances of the reciprocal accounts and present fairly the balances of inter-agency
receivables and payables in the financial statements.

Particularly, the Municipal Accountant request from the Provincial Accountant the
copies of the JEVs showing all the transactions recorded in the books of accounts of the
Province that should be taken up in the Municipality’s books of accounts.

We recommended that Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1 series of 2020 of the
Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG), Department of Budget and
Management (DBM), Department of Social Welfare and Development (DSWD),
Department of Labor and Employment (DOLE), Department of Trade and Industry
(DTI), Department of Agriculture (DA) and Department of Finance (DOF) be considered
to efficiently and effectively implement the social amelioration program like food
assistance program.

We further recommended that:

a. The Bids and Awards Committee Chairman, Vice-Chairman and its members
ensure provisions of Government Procurement Policy Board (GPPB) Circular 01-2020
dated April 6, 2020 are fully complied with particularly the online portal posting;
b. The Municipal Accountant account and submit to the Local Auditor the missing
disbursement voucher totaling P358,000.00 and document properly the transactions
paid through debit memo as prescribed in Sections 74 and 75 of Government
Accounting Manual for Local Government Units; and
c. The Sangguniang Bayan of the Municipality of Taysan observe the provisions of
Article 454(b) of Republic Act No. 7160 Implementing Rules and Regulations that a
fund can only be subject of supplemental budget once and not on several occasions
or thrice.

We recommended that the Municipality submit the necessary supporting
documents of those transactions where deficiencies are noted and detailed in the
Annex. Also, ensure that succeeding disbursements are completely documented
specially to those pertaining to PPAs which post-monitoring is essential to determine
if the goals are attained by the Municipality.

As to those inconsistencies noted, provide justification to identify the reasons
on those observations, summarized below, as follows:

• Discrepancies on the documents of the procurement of NFA rice
• Provision of various rates on the labor payroll for hauling of welfare goods
• Documentation relative to the provision for financial assistance

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor instruct Municipal Social Welfare
Development Officer to provide justification for the observations noted on the
implementation of SAP – ESP to the audit team. Likewise, ensure that the terms, rules
and regulations on the succeeding social amelioration programs are strictly followed
and implemented by the Municipality to ensure timely delivery of relief to the
constituents.

We recommended that the Local School Board strictly comply with the
provisions of Joint Circular No. 1, series of 2017 of the Department of Education,
Department of Budget and Management and Department of the Interior and Local
Government in order to ensure that Special Education Fund (SEF) will be utilized for
the intended purpose or according to the identified priority areas listed in the Annual
Implementation Plan (AIP), DepEd School Improvement Plan (SIP) and the Annual and
Supplemental Budget and presented in adherence to the said guidelines.

We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant consistently use the
prescribed method of accounting for the advance payment of Special Education Tax
illustratively shown in Item 2 (5) (1), Section 5, Chapter 3, Volume III of Government
Accounting Manual for Local Government Units.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure the efficient and effective
utilization of the 20% Development Fund which is primarily intended to fund projects
that shall contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and
environmental outcomes. Any delay in the completion of development projects being
implemented by the Municipality definitely slow down the community development or
realization of development objectives intended for the constituents.

We also recommended that any changes/reversions/realignments shall be
made pursuant to Articles 417 and Article 454 (b) of the Implementing Rules and
Regulations of RA 7160.

We further recommended that programs and projects be systematically
monitored and evaluated to enhance the efficiency of local government expenditures
and delivery of public services.

Based on the forgoing observations, we recommended that the Municipal
Mayor require the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office
(MDRRMO) implement the programs and projects of the Municipality under 70%
Mitigation Fund within the intended time to avoid delay in the delivery of services
and benefits to the constituents.

Likewise, direct also the MDRRMO to coordinate with the Municipal Budget Office and
Accounting Office for the reconciliation of balances reported in the Utilization Report
Summary. Also, see to it that in-kind donations are accounted and reported using the
required templates and forms as prescribed in the circular.

We recommended that the Municipality strictly observe the provisions of
Sections 321 and 336 of Republic Act No. 7160 and Articles 417 and 454(b)of the Act’s
Implementing Rules and Regulations and promote the Code’s objectives of improving
and systematizing methods, techniques, and procedures employed in the preparation
of the annual budget and supplemental budget and discourage any practice that
undermines the integration of planning and budgeting at the local level.

We recommended that the Municipality create its Local Finance Committee
pursuant to Section 316 of RA 7160 or the “Local Government Code of 1991’ which
shall be the: Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator, Municipal Budget
Officer and Municipal Treasurer.

We recommended that the Honorable Sangguniang Bayan consider the
observed inconsistent provisions of Article B of the Local Revenue Code of the
Municipality of Taysan with the provisions of RA 7160 or the “Local Government
Code of 1991” that render the provisions legality and enforceability questionable.

We recommended that the Sangguniang Bayan of the Municipality of Taysan
consider the provisions of Section 17 of RA 7160 mandating the basic services and
facilities that the Municipality shall efficiently and effectively provide. The Section
expressly mandated the Province to provide or implement social housing program not to
the Municipality.

Based on the foregoing, we recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the following:
a. The Municipal Treasurer conduct annually the physical count of all Property, Plant and Equipment and reconcile RPCPPE with the accounting records to fully identify the discrepancies noted. Complete all the fields required in the prescribed Reports on Physical Count and the necessary remarks. With regard to disposal, complete the necessary documents and perform the subsequent procedures in accordance with the Circular. Likewise, ensure that all PPEs are fully supported with the PPE Cards;
b. The Accounting Department submit copies of PPE Ledger Cards and Lapsing Schedule of PPE to the audit team for CY 2021 onwards. Have those records reconciled with the GSO and document the results to be the bases of adjustments, if any. Likewise, ensure that the Subsidiary Ledgers/Lapsing Schedule of PPE tally with the balances in the Financial Statement.; and
c. The Municipal Engineer assist the General Services Office and the Municipal Accounting Office in the conduct of the physical count and reconciliation of PPEs particularly to their Infrastructure Assets and Buildings and Other Structures accounts. Ensure that all PPEs in those groups of accounts are accounted and verified.
observations.managementAction:
Periodic physical count of properties was done for the CY 2020. The discrepancy of the balances of PPE on the financial statement was due to the overlooking of the columns pertaining to the balances per card and on hand per count instead, only the column of balances, quantity and status whether serviceable, unserviceable, donated, missing and stolen reflecting the values equivalent to the amount in the financial statement are filled out.
The Municipality used the RPCPPE format on the presentation of schedules of physical inventory of all properties. Moving forward, we will use the prescribed forms for each classification of properties and fill up the required columns as prescribed for the physical count of properties.
The concerned officers and staff have plan to conduct inspection and meeting to determine the appraise value of unserviceable properties to have an orderly and systematic approach of disposal
Currently the municipal treasurer’s office and the municipal accounting office are preparing property cards and PPE ledger cards, respectively. Regarding the lapsing schedules, these were prepared and presented in the bound Financial Statement for CY 2020. The accounting office ensures that the Lapsing schedule are reconciled with the balances in the Financial Statement.
Relative to above audit issues, we will exert more effort to comply with your recommendations and submit the required schedules in the audit of CY 2021. Closed coordination and communication will be done with the concerned officers and staff particularly for the Infrastructure Assets, Buildings and Other Structures.
Thank you very much for your usual support.

For the remaining items under trust fund, as explained in the reply on the prior year AOM, early of year 2020 we have provided the Province with the breakdown of our ledger including the particulars/description of the balances. Due to this, we weren’t able to reconcile due to insufficient data/information from them. The remaining ₱30,000.00 is a current subsidy which is intended for the acquisition of computer set, thus not included in the letter of request.
The Municipality will continue to coordinate with the Provincial Office if there are other possible ways to remove the unreconciled items. As of to date we haven’t received reply from the Office of the Governor relative to our request, but we will make follow-ups.

observations.managementAction:
The Sangguniang Bayan as a legislative body of the Municipality passed Resolusyon Bilang 2013-045 entitled “Pagpapatibay ng Revised Revenue Code ng Taysan, Batangas” pursuant to the Local Government Code or RA 7160.
Resolusyon Bilang 2013-045 having been approved by the Sangguniang Bayan of Taysan was forwarded to the Sangguniang Panlalawigan for their review on December 18, 2013, in accordance with the provisions of RA 7160, otherwise known as the Local Government Code of 1991: “Section 56. Review of Component City and Municipal Ordinances or Resolutions by the Sangguniang Panlalawigan. – xxx (c) If the sangguniang panlalawigan finds that such an ordinance or resolution is beyond the power conferred upon the sangguniang panlungsod or sangguniang bayan concerned, it shall declare such ordinance or resolution invalid in whole or in part. The sangguniang panlalawigan shall enter its action in the minutes and shall advise the corresponding city or municipal authorities of the action it has taken. (d) If no action has been taken by the sangguniang panlalawigan within thirty (30) days after submission of such an ordinance or resolution, the same shall be presumed consistent with law and therefore valid. The Department of the Interior and Local Government, Provincial Legal Opinion No. 03 s. 2011, stated the following: “the Department had consistently opined that in interpreting Section 56 (d) of the Local Government Code, the phrase ‘no action has been taken’ should be construed as either approval or disapproval of the ordinance and not just any action by the reviewing sanggunian such as referral to a committee. If no action has been taken” by the sangguniang panlalawigan within thirty (30) days after submission to it of the municipal ordinance for review, the same shall be presumed consistent with law and therefore valid. The 30-day period has to reckoned from receipt of such ordinance by the sangguniang panlalawigan and should be construed mandatory. It is a well-established principle in statutory construction that when the time is specified for the execution of an official act and such time was designated in order to limit the power or authority of the officer or body, such time is considered mandatory.”

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure the efficient and effective utilization of the 20% Development Fund which is primarily intended to fund projects that shall contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes. Any delay in the completion of development projects being implemented by the Municipality definitely slow down the community development or realization of development objectives intended for the constituents. We also recommended that any changes/reversions/realignments shall be made pursuant to Articles 417 and Article 454 (b) of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of RA 7160.
observations.managementAction:
For the current CDF fund of the Municipality, we have implemented 68.02% or ₱16,967,593.59 of the planned projects (Total CDF for CY2020 is ₱24,943,500.00 for the CY2020) with 0.70% or ₱175,735.85 balances of completed projects which will be realigned to other efficient programs and projects. Relative to the remaining current CDF as of December 31, 2020 with a total amount of ₱8,151,642.26, ₱2,975,906.41 or 11.23% of the total current CDF were implemented in the months of March and April of CY2021 and for ₱5,000,000.00 or 20.05%, this is intended for the purchase of Lot for the proposed Municipality’s landfill, target to be implemented in 2nd Quarter of this year. The request for the authority to purchase the Lot for the said purpose has already been forwarded to SB.
The concerned officers of the Municipality exerted full effort to implement the planned programs and projects despite the risks during the pandemic. By means of following the health protocol and what are mandated by the IATF, the Municipality was able to implement almost 70% of the current community development fund.
Article 417 (a) which indicates that the savings are portions or balances as of any given point in the fiscal year of any programmed or allotted appropriation. Hence, the source of the supplemental budget can be funded from the appropriation of the fiscal year of current surplus. It also stated that fund can come from any programs or allotted appropriations which remain free of any obligation. It implies that the LGU can realign/revert current appropriation if it is not obligated.
Moreover, under the Proclamation No. 922-“Declaring a State of Public Health Emergency Throughout the Philippines” put the country to state of public emergency during the State of Calamity. This allows the LGU to make changes in the annual budget to provide the needs of its constituents. Thus the Sangguniang Bayan enacted Supplemental Budgets are enacted to cope up of the challenges given because of the Covid-19 Pandemic, Taal Eruption and the Typhoons faced by our Municipality.
Lastly, approved supplemental budgets are reviewed and approved by the Sangguinang Panlalawigan so that the enacted ordinances related to the budget follow the rules and regulations under the budgeting process.
For the unobligated amount of our 20% CDF continuing appropriation, almost 56% of its amount was our equity for our Proposed PRDP Project (₱11,053,250.00) which is already bid out as of April 18, 2021 and on process for the Issuance of No Objection Letter (NOL 2) prior to implementation. After almost 4 years of processing for this proposed project, we need this certain equity to remain intact since this is one of the project proposal requirements and to make this PRDP project possible and more reliable. We are certain that this project with title Improvement of Brgy. Sto Niño and Brgy Piña Farm-to-Market Road and the Construction of Bridge to be located at Brgys. Sto Nino and Pina with a scale of 3.95 kilometers in length with 5 meters carriageway will highly contribute to the socio-economic, environmental and infrastructure development for the Municipality as well as our constituents.
The remaining ₱400,000.00 or 1.68% balance of continuing appropriation was implemented in March of 2021, and other remaining balances are the totals of residues from the implemented projects which will be evaluated and realign to other efficient projects and programs.
Despite of the challenges brought by the pandemic, we were able to serve the public with the best of what we can and will continue to extend and provide them the help and services that they deserve to have and receive, And still as per your recommendation we will be more stringent and systematically monitored the implementation of our 20% Development Fund. Thank you very much for your usual support.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Honorable Sangguniang Bayan consider the observed inconsistent provisions of Article B of the Local Revenue Code of the Municipality of Taysan with the provisions of RA 7160 or the “Local Government Code of 1991” that render the provisions legality and enforceability questionable.
observations.managementAction:
The Sangguniang Bayan as a Legislative body of the Municipality passed Resolusyon Bilang 2013-045 entitled “Pagpapatibay ng Revised Revenue Code ng Taysan, Batangas” pursuant to the Local Government Code or RA 7160.
Resolusyon Bilang 2013-045 having been approved by the Sangguniang Bayan of Taysan was forwarded to the Sangguniang Panlalawigan for their review on December 18, 2013, in accordance with the provisions of RA 7160; otherwise known as the Local Government Code of 1991.
The Department of the Interior and Local Government, Provincial Legal Opinion No. 03 s. 2011, stated the following:
“the Department had consistently opined that in interpreting Section 56 (d) of the Local Government Code, the phrase ‘no action has been taken’ should be construed as either approval or disapproval of the ordinance and not just any action by the reviewing sanggunian such as referral to a committee. If no action has been taken” by the sangguniang panlalawigan within thirty (30) days after submission to it of the municipal ordinance for review, the same shall be presumed consistent with law and therefore valid. The 30-day period has to reckoned from receipt of such ordinance by the sangguniang panlalawigan and should be construed mandatory. It is a well-established principle in statutory construction that when the time is specified for the execution of an official act and such time was designated in order to limit the power or authority of the officer or body, such time is considered mandatory.”
Hence, since the law recognizes a situation where the Sangguniang Panlalawigan can just simply let the thirty (30) day period to lapse as anyway the law provides for its consequential legal effect, no liability will attach.
In line with the above stated provisions we would appreciate your concurrence in our interpretation of the rules and provisions of RA 7160 and DILG Legal Opinion which refers to the issues presented on AOM 005.
Thank you very much.

Grande P. Gutierrez bilang kinatawan ng Pamahalaang Bayan ng Taysan, na pumasok at lumagda sa isang kasunduan, ang Memorandum of Understanding, sa pagitan ng Pamahalaang Bayan ng Taysan at ng Social Housing Finance Corporation para sa Social Housing Program ng Pamahalaang Bayan ng Taysan” pursuant to Section 444 (b) (1)(vi) of the Local Government Code provides that the Municipal Mayor, as the chief executive of the local government unit upon authorization by the Sangguniang Bayan, represent the municipality in all its business transactions and sign on its behalf all bonds, contracts, and obligations, and such other documents made pursuant to law or ordinance.
Resolusyon Bilang 2020 – 075 having been approved by the Sangguniang Bayan of Taysan was forwarded to the Sangguniang Panlalawigan for their review in accordance with the provisions of RA 7160; otherwise known as the Local Government Code of 1991.
