used by schools and three parcels of land occupied by other government
agencies. Otherwise, coordinate with the Municipal Accountant to provide
disclosures in the Notes to Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the concerned agency officials to
evaluate the observations noted above and determine the courses of action to be taken
and be implemented by each department as compliance with COA Circular No. 2015-
008.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Treasurer to
effect the cancellation of the stale checks and the Municipal Accountant to make
proper adjustments in the municipality’s books of accounts to fairly present its
financial statements as of December 31, 2019.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the following:
• Municipal Treasurer continue coordinating with the Municipal Accountant to zero
out the unreconciled balance and present a reliable RPT and SET Receivables in
the financial statements; and
• Municipal Treasurer devise strategies to collect all delinquent real property taxes
and encourage the taxpayer to pay their dues on time. Conduct verification on
those outstanding balances to determine the accuracy of the receivables and its
collectability.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant reconcile the municipal
records with the province’s books of accounts to establish the correct balances of
the reciprocal accounts and present fairly the balances of inter-agency receivables
and payables in the financial statements.
Particularly, the Municipal Accountant request from the Provincial Accountant
the copies of the JEVs showing all the transactions recorded in the books of
accounts of the Province that should be taken up in the Municipality’s books of
accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant draw Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to
make the necessary adjustment in the accounting error committed in the recording of
advance payment of real property tax and special education tax in CY 2019 and fairly
present the deferred accounts in the financial statements of the Municipality as of
December 31, 2019.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant restate the Notes to Financial
Statements No. 25 and 29.1 to correct the observed deficiencies and comply with the
stated objective of providing additional information to the intended users of the
financial statements and reduce the level of rounding of the amounts of the accumulated
depreciation of the property, plant and equipment in conformity with the accounting
principle of materiality to provide a more reliable and significant information pertaining to
these accounts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor immediately instruct the Municipal
Treasurer to remit the real property tax share to all the barangays to augment the
limited financial resources and to allow the immediate use of the fund for regular
expenses or priority projects.
Likewise, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer to strictly comply with the
provision of Article 362 (b) of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of the Local
Government Code of 1991.

We recommended that the Municipality reconcile employees’ records with the
GSIS, PhilHealth and PAG-IBIG to identify the affected employees on the unremitted
balances and immediately remit the amounts due to these agencies and identify the
cause/s of the over remittance for some accounts and make the proper adjustments
to avoid the incurrence of unnecessary financial expenses in the form of imposed
penalties. Likewise, review the taxes withheld from employees, suppliers and
contractors to ensure that all withheld taxes are remitted on the prescribed period to
avoid penalties and surcharges.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the following:
• The Municipal Accountant immediately submit the liquidation reports if not yet
submitted and the check to the source agency for refund of the unexpended
balance. See to it that future fund transfers are fully implemented within the time
frame to avoid delay in liquidation; and
• The Head of the Municipal Planning and Development Office coordinate with
the concerned department heads to enforce the utilization and implementation
of those projects that are not yet implemented while pursue the continuation of
those ongoing utilization. Likewise, see to it that their respective time frames
and schedules are strictly monitored and evaluated to avoid delays in its
implementation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the concerned department
heads to immediately initiate action to enforce the settlement of the disallowances in
accordance with the COA Revised Rules of Procedure on the Settlement of Accounts
(RRSA).

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant impose the liquidated
damages to the two (2) contractors or deduct the corresponding amount from the
contractors’ billing, retention money or other securities posted by the contractor
whichever is convenient to the Municipality.
We further recommended that the Municipal Engineer implement a more stringent
monitoring of all existing infrastructure projects to ensure that provisions of
infrastructure contracts were followed in the prosecution of the municipal
infrastructure projects and breach or violations are addressed immediately with the
imposition of corresponding sanctions or penalties.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure the efficient and effective
utilization of the 20% Development Fund which is primarily intended to fund projects
that shall contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development. We
further recommended the strict observance of Article 454(d) of the Implementing
Rules and Regulations of RA 7160 that disbursements from special accounts shall
proceed from itemized appropriation which shall be for specific development
projects/activities embodied in the local development plan and/or public investment
program formulated and prioritized by the Local Development Council and approved
by the Sanggunian concerned.
We likewise recommended that changes in the Annual Budget be implemented
in accordance with Article 417 and 454 (b) of the Implementing Rules and Regulations
of RA No. 7160 and the Local Development Council prepare and submit to the Local
Finance Committee the Annual Investment Program which is the annual slice of the
Local Development Investment Program and indicative of yearly expenditure
requirements of the LGUs’ programs, projects and activities (PPAs) to be integrated
into the annual budget.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
1. Require the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (MDRRM)
enforce the implementation of the programs and projects of the Municipality
under70% Mitigation Fund, Continuing Appropriation and Trust Liability –
MDRRMF. Have those programs completed within the intended time to avoid delay
in the delivery of services and benefits to the constituents; and
2. Require the Municipal Budget Officer monitor the utilization of 5% LDRRMF and
ensure that program and projects are aligned with their intended appropriation.

We recommended that the Municipality strictly comply with the provisions of Joint
Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 of Philippine Commission on Women,
Department of the Interior and Local Government, Department of Budget and
Management and National Economic and Development Authority in the
implementation of the mainstreaming of gender perspectives in its locality and in the
selection of gender issues or mandates that the Municipality intends to address in
the implementation of gender and development programs/projects/activities.
We further recommended stringent compliance with the provision of Section 5(2) of
the Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2016-01 dated January 12, 2016 of the
Philippine Commission on Women, Department of the Interior and Local Government,
Department of Budget and Management and National Economic and Development
Authority that the Municipality shall submit her Annual Gender and Development
(GAD) Plan and Budget (GPB) to the Provincial Planning and Development Office
(PPDO) of the Provincial Government to ensure the alignment of the municipal GAD
PPAs with the priorities of the province and then to the DILG Provincial Office for its
review and endorsement to the concerned LGU for incorporation in her annual
budget to be enacted by her Local Sanggunian.

We recommended that the Municipality fully implement the Municipal Solid
Waste Management Plan covering the period CY 2017 to 2027pursuant to the
provisions of Republic Act No. 9003 or “Philippine Ecological Solid Waste
Management Act of 2000” that espouses on the implementation of the 3R or the
reduce, reuse and recycle concept with focus on reduction of pollution and waste
generation.

We recommended that the Municipality strictly observed the provisions of Sections 321
and 336 of Republic Act No. 7160 and Article 417 454(b)of the Act Implementing Rules
and Regulations and promote the Code objectives of discouraging careless preparation
of the annual budget or any practice that undermines the integration of planning and
budgeting at the local level.

We recommended that the Municipal Local Development Council and the
Sangguniang Bayan of the Municipality of Taysan explain the observed
inconsistencies between the prepared and approved Supplemental Investment
Programs of the Municipality in CY 2019. We further recommended that the Local
Development Council provided to the Commission copies of Supplemental
Investment Programs pertaining to Sangguniang Bayan Resolution No, 2019-012;
2019-014; 2019-N46; 2019-N57; 2019-N82; 2019-N127 and 2019-N141.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a) Instruct the Office of the Municipal Accountant and Treasurer’s Office to
evaluate the causes of discrepancies to ascertain the accuracy of the
PPE balances in the financial statements. Likewise, submit justification
to give details on the noted increase of the total variance between the
Accounting and Treasurer’s records for the year 2018 as compared to
the 2017 discrepancy;
b) If applicable, address the issue of lack of manpower in the Property
Division spearheaded by the Acting Municipal Treasurer to strengthen
monitoring controls over the properties of the Municipality;
c) Instruct the Acting Municipal Treasurer to investigate on the cause of
the missing or un-located properties. Identify who are accountable by
tracing the recipient from the Memorandum Receipt (MR) or the
Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE). The accountable
officer may request for the relief from property accountability if the loss
was not due to his/her negligence but if found that due care as that of a
good father was not performed, he/she can be held liable for the
replacement cost of such property or equipment. Refer to Sections 151
and 152 of COA Circular 92-386 on the requirements for application for
relief, if applicable;
d) Require the Acting Municipal Treasurer to prepare the Inventory and
Inspection Report of Unserviceable Properties as basis in the disposal
of the unserviceable properties. Direct the Chairman of the Committee
on Disposal, if any, to initiate/facilitate the immediate disposal of those
properties in accordance with COA Circular No. 92-386 dated October
20, 1992 in order not to expose those properties to elements that cause
further deterioration or loss of salvaged value. This will also enable the
municipality to efficiently use/utilize the space occupied by these
unserviceable properties and to generate of additional income in favor
of the agency. Likewise, to instruct the Municipal Accountant to drop
the carrying amount of the unserviceable properties and make the
necessary adjusting entries in the books of accounts upon disposal of
the said property; and
e) Cause the Municipal Accountant to compute the depreciation of the
noted PPE accounts and make the necessary adjustments on the
books. Henceforth, depreciation must be computed for all the PPE
accounts of the Municipality periodically to ensure fair presentation of
depreciation and related accounts in the financial statements so as not
to mislead the users of the financial information.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
Municipal Accountant -
a. Reconcile the difference between the balances of Real Property Tax (RPT)
Receivable and Special Education Tax (SET) Receivable;
b. Conduct a regular reconciliation of records with the Acting Municipal
Treasurer pertaining to the set-up RPT and SET Receivables and Deferred
Income Tax at the beginning of the period and the consistent adjustment of
these accounts upon collection;
c. Maintain the necessary subsidiary ledgers for the RPT and SET
Receivables in order to validate the balances of the accounts presented in
the financial statements and to ensure accuracy and reliability of the
balances reported monthly; and
d. Review all entries reflected in the general journal so as to ensure that all
recorded adjustments are correct and complete. Effect the necessary
adjustment/s, if any.
Acting Municipal Treasurer -
a. Conduct reconciliation of records with the Assessor’s Office in order to
have a uniform and updated taxpayers’ records for more accurate RPT/SET
collectibles; and
b. Monitor payment of real property tax due for the current year and exhaust
all efforts and devise strategies to collect all delinquent real property taxes
and encourage the tax payer to pay their dues on time. Conduct verification
on those outstanding balances to determine the accuracy of the
receivables and its collectibility.
Municipal Assessor to expedite the updating/cleansing of their records for the
uniform and accurate reporting of the real properties to be assessed. Thereafter,
submit the Monthly Report of Assessment of Real Properties and Individual Tax
Payers Assessment to the Municipal Treasurer’s Office for reconciliation of their
individual records.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant and her staff to continuously coordinate with the Provincial
Accountant and reconcile their records on their reciprocal receivables/payables in
the books of the Province to establish the correct balances in the corresponding
subsidiary ledgers and to ascertain the legality and validity of both claims so both
parties may make the necessary adjustments on their respective books of
accounts, if warranted. We further recommended that immediate reconciliation be
done every time a transaction occurs to avoid the recurrence of unreconciled
discrepancies between both books and accounts.
Likewise, effect the immediate settlement of obligations of each fund for a fair
presentation of the cash position for the three funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the following:
a) Office of the Municipal Assessor to prioritize the continuous application for the
Original Certificate of Title (OCT) through Special Patent of the parcels of land
used for public purposes other than educational pursuant to DENR
Administrative Order No. 2016-21 dated June 30, 2016 to protect the
government properties to possible risk of court litigation in the future;
b) Acting Municipal Treasurer to coordinate with the Assessor’s Office on the
specific determination of the parcels of land to have an updated Report on the
Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) and submit to the
Audit Team for proper evaluation;
c) Municipal Accountant to make the necessary adjusting entries corresponding
the appraised value of the parcels of land with Original Certificate of Title
(OCT) already issued through Special Patent on January 2019. Exert additional
effort to expedite the application for the transfer of title of the two parcels of
land acquired in CY 2018; and
d) Sangguniang Bayan to discuss and evaluate the best possible actions
pertaining the real properties which are still in the name of the Municipality but
primarily for educational purposes, taking into considerations the above
mentioned regulations specifically applicable to public schools.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor aim to reduce unnecessary delays
in project implementation and strictly effect the necessary processes to
expedite the completion of the not-yet started and on-going projects to allow
the early enjoyment of benefits by the general public and the full utilization of
the funds received from National Government Agencies (NGAs).

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a) In coordination with the Sangguniang Bayan through the insights of other
Department Heads, Acting Municipal Treasurer and the Designated Market
Supervisor, make a thorough and joint study on the present market
operation in order to justify the need to immediately prioritize the revision
of the 2001 Taysan Market Code in order to improve the income generating
capacity of this economic enterprise and to utilize the subsidized funds for
other relevant purposes.
b) Instruct the Municipal Treasurer to institute actions for the intensive
collection of delinquency on monthly rental of stall fees and impose the
sanctions against delinquent stallholders;
c) Require the Market Administrator to accept qualified and new lessees for
the unoccupied/vacant stalls in the market;
d) Instruct the Human Resource Management Officer to review the plantilla
positions for possible hiring of personnel who can assist in the operation
of the market specifically in the collection of rentals;
e) Execute a contract of lease embodied in a public instrument for every
public market stallholder pursuant to COA Circular No. 88-282A and the
Taysan Market Code to ensure that interests of both parties are protected,
ambiguities and/or misunderstandings are avoided and peaceful operation
of public market in the Municipality is maintained; and
f) Instruct the Office of the Municipal Engineer to put labels/markers showing
the appropriate classification of each market stall to facilitate easier
monitoring of the operations and compliance of the stallholders.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to
use the Advances to Contractors account when paying and recording the 15
percent advances to contractors and make the necessary adjusting entries for the
noted deficiency, if still applicable. Likewise, strictly adhere with Section 4.2
Annex E of the Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations of R.A. 9184 on the
posting of the irrevocable standby letter of credit of equal amount to support the
15 percent advance payment which is a mandatory documentary requirement in
the payment of infrastructure projects.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
Municipal Engineer to -
• Use the PERT/CPM analysis in estimating the actual number of working days
required to complete the project and in granting extensions; and
• Perform planning and detailed engineering including site inspections to
anticipate and consider interruptions that may probably arise during
construction in estimating the contract time of infrastructure projects.
MPDC/BAC Chairman to ensure that -
• The programs/projects/activities are properly planned before undergoing
bidding process to avoid problems during the implementation stage that may
cause delay and to assist the Municipal Engineer on project monitoring; and
• Documents submitted by the bidders are complete and properly filled up to
establish the propriety of the bidding process. Require the contractors to
amend/correct the deficient Performance Security for the protection of the
Municipality and for their faithful compliance on the completion of the
projects.
Nevertheless, we recommend that the concerned officials consistently monitor the
infrastructure projects of the Municipality to ensure that projects are completed
within the contract time to avoid delays resulting in deferred benefits to the public.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a) Direct the BAC and its TWG to -
• Submit justification on the failure to conduct proper and correct
procedures in the bid evaluation and post-qualification stages that resulted
in the submission of inaccurate NFCC due to the non-validation of the
contractor’s list of on-going projects/awarded but not yet started and the
award of contracts in spite non-compliance with the documentary
requirements;
• Study the adequacy of the manpower and equipment in view of all projects
to be awarded to the winning bidder’s and not on an individual project only,
in cases where the lowest bidder in several projects have the same or
overlapping/coinciding implementation period. This is to order the winning
contractor to increase its manpower and/or equipment to be employed in
the project awarded to it or to disqualify them from obtaining several award
or contracts;
• Strictly observe/monitor bidders with on-going projects with other government
agencies not disclosed in the Statement of On-going and Completed projects to
comply with the bidding requirements and consequently avoid delay in project
accomplishment and/or substandard quality of work; and
• Verify, validate and confirm all statements made and documents submitted
by the bidder in order to determine whether the bidder concerned complies
with and is responsive to all the requirements and conditions specified in
the bidding documents.
b) Require the Municipal Engineer to –
• Consider re-evaluation of estimate of projects duration based on
experience from actual completion of previous projects and reduce the
stipulated contract time, if proper. This would allow the Municipality to
implement more projects during the year given the shorter time provided to
the contractors to complete the projects; and
• Strictly and closely monitor all projects simultaneously implemented by the
contractors, prepare an individual status report per project and if there are
delays, require the contractor for payment of liquidated damages.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:
Municipal Engineer to –
• Enforce the rectification on the noted deficiencies/damage on the
project and submit documents to support the action taken; and
• Invariably comply with Section 62 of Revised IRR of RA No. 9184
requiring the issuance of Certificate of Final Acceptance to the
contractor one year from project completion and upon submission of
the warranty security by the contractor for all infrastructure projects to
be implemented by the Municipality.
Bids and Awards Committee and Technical Working Group to –
• Strictly adhere with the pertinent provisions of Section 13 of the IRR of
R.A. 9184 in inviting observers in all stages of the procurement
process;
• Provide explanation for the inconsistencies noted and to immediately
submit all the lacking documents to the Auditor concerned as basis for
the next audit action and to check the propriety of the award of contract
and for further evaluation of the transaction to avoid suspension/
disallowance in audit; and
• Validate first all the eligibility documents submitted by the bidders
before awarding contracts to bonafide contractors. Otherwise, enforce
strictly the sanctions against the erring contractors, BAC/TWG and
other concerned officials for lapses committed on ensuing
procurements.
The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to refrain/stop the
processing of claims without complete documentation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
a) Bids and Awards Committee, BAC Secretariat and Technical Working
Group to explain in writing, the reason for pushing thru with the
proceeding/bidding despite “ineligibility” of the bidder and the concerned
officials to submit the lacking eligibility documents to the Audit Team for
further evaluation and ensure completeness of the bidding documents to
establish the propriety of the bidding process;
b) Municipal Accountant to deduct the 10 percent retention on payments to
contractor to cover for possible uncorrected discovered defects and third
party liabilities for infrastructure projects up to 50 percent completion of
the works if the project is within schedule, otherwise, it must be
continuously imposed;
c) Municipal Engineer in coordination with the Municipal Planning and
Development Coordinator to provide the necessary actions in order to fully
operate the potable water supply project so that the desired results will be
fully achieved; and
d) Municipal Engineer to comply with the rules and regulations as required by
DPWH Department Order No. 197 series of 2016 in the computation/
preparation of Approved Budget of the Contract (ABC) for all the
prospective projects of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor to:
a. Direct Municipal Planning and Development Officer and/or the Municipal
Engineer to optimally utilize the 20% Local Development Fund in order to
deliver the benefits of desirable social, economic and environmental outcomes
to the constituents which is essential in the attainment of the constitutional
objective;
b. Require the Municipal Engineer to comply with Section 62 of Revised IRR of
RA No. 9184 on the strict monitoring and issuance of Certificate of Final
Acceptance to the contractor one year from project completion and upon
submission of the warranty security by the contractor for all infrastructure
projects to be implemented by the Municipality;
c. Instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to ensure that projects under the 20%
Development Fund (DF) are included in the Annual Investment Plan (AIP) of the
Municipality and shall partake the nature of investment/capital expenditures and shall
contribute to the attainment of desirable socio-economic development and
environmental management outcomes
d. Submit justification/explanation on the delay/non-implementation of the priority
development projects listed in the CY 2018 20% Development Plan in spite of the
availability of funds;
e. Require the Bids and Awards Committee and Technical Working Group to -
strictly adhere with the pertinent provisions of Section 13 of the IRR of R.A. 9184 in
inviting observers in all stages of the procurement process.

We reiterated our recommendation that the Municipal Mayor instruct the
Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer to:
a) carefully plan on how to maximize the utilization of the LDRRM Funds and
to accelerate their implementation in order to achieve the goal/target of the
Municipality regarding disaster relief, recovery, and risk reduction and
management;
b) Submit justification on the continuous minimal utilization of the LDRRM
Funds and the status of the unutilized funds;
c) Ensure that the all charges to the 5 percent LDRRMF are aligned to the
Disaster Prevention and Preparedness goals of the Municipality. Likewise,
direct the Municipal Budget Officer to appropriate funds for the MDRRM
Office to avoid charging non-priority expenditures to the calamity fund.

We recommended the Municipal Mayor instruct the GAD Focal Person to:
a) Ensure the proper mainstreaming of GAD related activities in all department/
offices of the Municipality;
b) Secure first the Certificate of Endorsement of the Annual Gender and
Development (GAD) Plan and Budget (GPB) from the DILG Provincial Office
before incorporating to the Municipality’s Annual Budget to properly ascertain
the relevance of the gender issues identified and gender-responsiveness of
the PPAs indicated in the GPB;
c) Properly monitor and report regularly the status of implementation of the
activities stated in the Gender and Development Plan in order to address the
gender issues. Ensure that the programs and projects included in the GAD
Plan are properly followed and other factors that would affect the
implementation must be considered to avoid problems when it comes to
implementation; and
d) Attend training/seminar on how to administer the Harmonized Gender and
Development Guidelines (HGDG) tool. Thereafter, gradually increase the
gender-responsiveness of the Municipality’s programs and projects through
attribution using the HGDG tool.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Human Resource
Management Officer (HRMO):
a) To document the hiring of services of the job order personnel through an
individual contract of service, memorandum of agreement or job order
indicating the duties, responsibilities work to be done during the period,
together with the required stipulations/conditions as stated under Civil Service
Commission (CSC) Memorandum Circular (MC) No. 17 s. 2002 dated June 24,
2002 with clarifications embodied in CSC MC No. 24 s. 2002 dated November
12, 2002; and
b) In coordination with the department heads to assess its existing organizational
structure so that the ideal number of plantilla personnel will be the required
number of manpower in the respective offices of the municipality, to avoid
undue number of job order personnel.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to:
a) In coordination with the HRMO to regularly reconcile employees’ records at GSIS,
PhilHealth and PAG-IBIG to identify the affected employees on the unremitted
balances and immediately remit the amounts due to these agencies;
b) Identify the cause/s of the over remittance for some accounts and make the proper
adjustments; and
c) Remit the due GSIS loan amortizations pursuant to Section 14.2, Rule III of the
Implementing Rules and Regulations of Government Service Insurance Act of 1997
or R.A. No. 8291 to avoid the incurrence of unnecessary financial expenses in the
form of imposed penalties.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, as the chairperson of the
Municipal Council for the Protection of Children (MCPC):
a) Instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to allocate 1 percent share from IRA for
LCPC as provided in DILG MC No. 2012-120 dated July 4, 2012 for the
implementation LCPC;
b) Direct the members of the Council to prepare the Local Development Plan for
Children (LDPC) and Local Investment Plan for Children (LIPC), and maximize
utilization of the allocated LCPC Fund to identified programs, projects and
activities (PPAs) provided for in DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2012-120
dated July 4, 2012 to ensure full strengthening and implementation of the
LCPC; and
c) Require the Municipal Social Welfare Officer, being the focal person, to ensure
that the identified PPAs geared for promotion of rights and welfare of children
are carried out effectively and efficiently and continuously monitor the
implementation of children’s programs and projects in the locality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Office of the Human Resource
Management and the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to discontinue the practice of
allowing deductions on monthly loan amortizations of government/private lending
institutions and capital contribution to employees’ association from the personnel’s
payroll other than those mandated by law. Strictly comply with the GAA provision that
monthly take-home pay of employee shall not be lower than Five Thousand Pesos (P5,000.00).

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the: • designated GSO to dig deep additional information to detect the unidentified, missing and stolen properties. As for the stolen properties, the municipal officials/ employees designated accountable/ responsible based on the Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE).
observations.managementAction: The designated GSO submitted additional information to the Audit Team records pertaining some of the stolen properties. As for the missing PPEs, the municipal officials/ employees designated accountable/ responsible based on the Acknowledgement Receipt for Equipment (ARE) and requested for assistance for the location of the missing properties. The designated GSO furnished the Department Heads copies of their accountabilities of government properties and requested for information of the identified missing properties such as location and status. As regards to the stolen items, the Municipality will make necessary follow-up and actions to secure current status of their request for relief of accountability. While for the increase of P6,802,097.39 discrepancies, the Municipal Accountant stated that she will furnish the Treasurer’s Office copies of PPE schedule that will be presented in the Quarterly and Annual FS of the Municipality to ensure reconciliation with the latter’s records. The Designated GSO also submitted copy of reconciliation of the noted discrepancies. Likewise, the Management has created a disposal committee/team which will undertake the necessary procedure and documentation of the proper disposal of valueless/ unserviceable properties. Lastly, the Municipal Accountant asserted that her office will be more cautious in the presentation of depreciation schedule and will ensure that all depreciation expenses will be recognized to fairly present the carrying value of the Property Plant and Equipment. Depreciation for the noted PPEs was provided in CY 2019.
observations.reason: Despite the presence of unidentified beginning balances, missing and stolen PPEs, the designated GSO submitted to the Audit Team information to detect the unidentified, missing and stolen properties. As for the stolen PPEs, the GSO furnished the Department Heads copies of their accountabilities and requested for assistance for the location of the identified missing properties such as location and status. For the increase of P6,802,097.39 discrepancies, the Municipal Accountant will furnish the Treasurer’s Office copies of PPE schedule that will be presented in the Quarterly and Annual FS of the Municipality to ensure reconciliation with the latter’s records. The Management created a disposal committee/team to undertake the necessary procedure and documentation of the proper disposal of valueless/unserviceable properties. Depreciation for the noted PPEs was provided in CY 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant and her staff to continuously coordinate with the Provincial Accountant and reconcile their records on their reciprocal receivables/payables in the books of the Province to establish the correct balances in the corresponding subsidiary ledgers and to ascertain the legality and validity of both claims so both parties may make the necessary adjustments on their respective books of accounts, if warranted. We further recommended that immediate reconciliation be done every time a transaction occurs to avoid the recurrence of unreconciled discrepancies between both books and accounts. Likewise, effect the immediate settlement of obligations of each fund for a fair presentation of the cash position for the three funds.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant coordinated the recording/ posting of collections on loan and the loan payments were checked complete. Also, subsidiary ledgers were prepared by the Municipal Accountant. Copies of subsiduary ledgers and statement of liquidation were submitted by the Accounting Office as a proof of their justification.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant coordinated the recording of loan payments and prepared subsidiary ledgers. A meeting was conducted last May 22, 2019 attended by the Provincial Treasurer’s Office, Provincial Accounting Office, Municipal Treasurers and Municipal Accountants of different local government units including Taysan and one of the topics discussed was the reconciliation of Due to and Due from LGUs accounts. It was conferred and agreed upon that reconciliation will be done every end of the quarter which they think may lead to a more reliable, valid and correct year-end balances.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to: • analyze and review the details of the difference of the Loans Payable and loan payments; • check if there is lacking in the loan release; and • prepare the complete documentation of the payments on loan; • disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the current portion of the loan.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant prepared the complete documentation of the payments on loan; Complete documentation was prepared by the Municipal Accountant. Also, the loan release was checked complete. The complete disclosures were provided under Notes 10 and 16 of the Notes to Financial Statements for the current and non-current portion of the loan.
observations.reason: Loan release was checked complete. Subsidiary Ledger for the loan accounts were prepared by the Municipal Accountant. Complete documentation of the payments on loan were prepared. Sufficient disclosures were provided under Notes 10 and 16 of the Notes to Financial Statements for the current and non-current portion of the loan.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive thru the Municipal Mayor aim to maximize the utilization of the 20% Development Fund released the Municipality to ensure that the benefits will be realized to the constituents and to coordinate with the National Agencies, Barangays and Province which may have projects that might coincide with the planned project of the Municipality; have proper coordination among the project beneficiaries and the officials involved in the planning, execution and monitoring of the projects; clear identification and description of specific projects to be implemented; and ensure consistency of projects in the AIP and in the budget. Also to refrain from charging projects in the 20% Development Fund that are not allowed on the JMC 2017-1 and those projects not in the AIP and not in the budget.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality implemented some priority programs/projects and several projects for CY 2018 were implemented on the next calendar year. The Municipality ensured that the transactions, the Municipal Budget Officer prepared Supplemental Budget/s as needed for the implementation of the 20% DF. The Municipality also presented the list of projects and programs for the statutory appropriation of 20% DF. Also, procedures for realignment of budget were approved through resolution. The Municipality will reprogram some projects and will implement/defer some projects for CY 2019.
observations.reason: Some priority projects were implemented in CY 2018 while majority of the projects were implemented on the next calendar year (CY 2019). Some funds were reprogrammed. Some charges to the 20% DF such as the Clean and Green Program were discussed and reiterated. The Municipality still failed to maximize the 20% DF for CY 2018 with unutilized balance ₱33,300,637.28 of the total statutory appropriations. The Municipal Budget Officer ensured supplemental budgets were prepared and implemented, but many projects were not realized within the year.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer to: • carefully plan on how to maximize the utilization of the LDRRM Funds and to accelerate their implementation in order to achieve the goal/target of the Municipality regarding disaster relief, recovery, and risk reduction and management; • submit justification on the continuous minimal utilization of the LDRRM Funds and the status of the unutilized funds; • ensure that the all charges to the 5 percent LDRRMF are aligned to the Disaster Prevention and Preparedness goals of the Municipality; and • provide the necessary and complete personnel under the LDRRMO so that the LDRRM functions can be effectively and efficiently managed.
observations.managementAction: The MDRRMO presented their projects and activities in MDRRMC meetings. The MDRRMO also prepared updated LDRRM Investment Plan. The Municipality purchased rescue and recovery tents for every barangay and issued rescue vehicles to four barangays. Re-planning was made to procure rescue equipment like Back Hoe, Dump Truck, Hailing Device Extricating Tools, Radio Communication and other disaster preparedness equipment and devices. The MDRRMO explained the use of office supplies and meals for MDRRMC meetings and Oplan Undas, and provided justification that purchased appliances were part of portable cooking kits/community kitchen and evacuation center standards. The Municipality ensured some procurements and reprogramming for CY 2019.
observations.reason: The Municipality presented updated LDRRM Investment Plan and conducted procurements and acquisitions for certain equipment and supplies. However, utilization of CY 2018 LDRRMF was still minimal (only ₱7,961,312.33 utilized) leaving ₱12,571,085.28 unutilized. Some charges to LDRRMF were deemed ineligible by audit but Management provided justifications (meals, office supplies, appliances) citing their utility in disaster operations. Staffing capacity for MDRRMO remains limited.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local School Board prioritize and implement projects and activities to address school needs, ensure proper planning for school priorities, and ensure expenditures are charged to SEF as provided under Section 272 of RA 7160 and Joint Circular No. 1 s. 1998 of DECS, DBM and DILG. Also submit justification why the amount ₱633,565.00 was charged against the SEF despite provisions of the Joint Circular and ensure adequacy of allotments for disbursements.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality implemented several building projects in CY 2018 out of the Special Education Fund to address lack of classrooms. Various priority projects were implemented in CY 2019. Also obligations were reviewed and adjustments done. The Local School Board submitted justification and requested reconsideration on the audit observation.
observations.reason: The Municipality ensured priority school projects were implemented, and validated charges. The Local School Board submitted justification and requested reconsideration on the said audit observation. Several priority projects implemented in CY 2019 addressed concerns.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Accountant to: • ensure that cash advances for seminars and other expenses are liquidated within the prescribed number of days after its purpose has been served; • refrain from processing disbursement vouchers for a new cash advance of accountable officers until their previous cash advance has been settled; • exercise utmost diligence in monitoring liquidation of cash advances; and • ensure that cash advances are granted to the actual payees.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant enforced monitoring schedules, prepared monthly schedule of liquidated cash advances and initiated sufficient reminders to accountable officers. The Municipal Accountant ensured that cash advances for CY 2018 were liquidated and that new advances were only granted after previous ones were liquidated. For CY 2018, cash advances were granted only to accountable employees/officers and were liquidated.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant prepared monthly schedules related to Cash Advances and initiated reminders to accountable officers to liquidate. For CY 2018, cash advances were granted only to accountable employees/officers and previous advances were liquidated before granting new ones.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure adherence to the complete structure and positions of the LGU GFPS provided for in the MCW IRR; conduct capacity building of all members in GAD sensitivity training (GST), gender-responsive planning and budgeting, gender analysis and gender audit; facilitate establishment of GAD Database; conduct early planning and budgeting for GAD Plan and Budget (GPB) and forward to DILG Regional Office for review and approval; conduct meetings to monitor implementation of GAD activities; and assess compliance of institutional mechanisms and provide incentives for outstanding GAD PPAs.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality complied to the complete structure and positions of the LGU GFPS by issuing Executive Order No. 2018-017 on the Reorganization of the Municipal GFPS. Capacity building conducted on November 30 to December 1, 2018 in GAD sensitivity training (GST), gender-responsive planning & budgeting, gender analysis and gender audit participated by LGU employees and officials. The GAD Focal Person collated data and documents for the GAD Database and forwarded the early planned GPB for CY 2018 to PPDO on March 31, 2017 and to DILG Provincial Office on April 20, 2017 for review and endorsement. Follow-ups were made on the endorsement and coordination pursued. The Municipality included GAD activities in their programs and committed to continue building the database and secure endorsement.
observations.reason: The Municipality adhered to the GFPS structure and conducted capacity building and initiated the GAD Database and submitted GPB for review. However, the Certificate of Endorsement from DILG Provincial Office for CY 2018 GPB was not received. Some GAD activities remained unimplemented and the HGDG tool was not yet administered due to limited training attendance.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal Engineer and the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to ensure posting of public notice on all infrastructure projects to promote public awareness on the use of government funds as provided in COA Circular No. 2013-004.
observations.managementAction: Posting of public notice on infrastructure projects pursuant to the provisions and format under COA Circular No. 2013-004 was observed by the Municipality. The Municipal Engineer ensured posting of public notices and information boards on awarded and implemented projects.
observations.reason: The Municipality observed the posting of public notices on the infrastructure projects pursuant to COA Circular No. 2013-004.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the General Services Officer-Designate to meticulously and judiciously plan and incorporate procurements in the Annual Procurement Plan (APP) and adopt competitive/public bidding where applicable and ensure that only emergency purchases and ordinary or regular supplies not available in Procurement Services and within the threshold are purchased through shopping.
observations.managementAction: The General Services Officer-Designate encouraged departmental consolidation of requirements and assisted departments to adopt competitive procurement and incorporate needs in the APP. The Municipality ensured that only emergency purchases were made via shopping and promoted inclusion of PRs in the succeeding quarter for consolidation.
observations.reason: The Municipality encouraged consolidation and inclusion in the APP. Some departments still procured by shopping for immediate needs; however, efforts were made to include PRs in the succeeding quarter and follow procurement procedures.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to prepare and immediately submit the required reports and to strictly adhere with the rules on disclosing information in the notes to financial statements in compliance with PPSAS. Also to prepare the reconciliation of actual amounts on a comparable basis as presented in the SCBAA to the total revenues and expenses presented in the Statement of Financial Performance and the Reconciliation of surplus/(deficit) per Statement of Financial Performance to net cash flows from operating activities.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant prepared the necessary reconciliations disclosed in the Notes to Financial Statements and ensured compliance with PPSAS. The Municipality presented reconciliations and provided clarifications on non-cash items and timing differences.
observations.reason: The Municipal Accountant prepared the reconciliations and provided the disclosures in the Notes to Financial Statements and ensured that the amounts on SCBAA were reconciled to the Statement of Financial Performance and cash flows.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor aim to reduce unnecessary delays in project implementation and strictly effect necessary processes to expedite completion of not-yet started and ongoing projects to allow early enjoyment of benefits and full utilization of funds received from National Government Agencies (NGAs).
observations.managementAction: The Municipality identified causes of delays (late release of authority to purchase, supply shortages, strict DSWD guidelines) and followed up with NGA offices for approvals. Some projects were re-scheduled and procurement processes were expedited; training participant lists were revised upon DSWD approval. Delayed completion of LGSF-AM projects due to site conditions and contractor requests for extension were documented.
observations.reason: Delays were due to late release of authority, supply shortages (NFA rice), and compliance with NGA requirements. Some projects remained on-going or were implemented in CY 2019; management took corrective actions and requested authority/approvals where needed.

observations.recommendation: We recommended the Municipal Mayor instruct the GAD Focal Person to: ensure proper mainstreaming of GAD activities in all departments/offices; secure Certificate of Endorsement of the GPB from DILG Provincial Office; monitor and report regularly the status of implementation of GAD Plan activities; attend training on the HGDG tool and administer it to increase gender-responsiveness.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality issued Executive Order reorganizing GFPS, conducted GAD trainings, and initiated establishment of GAD Database. The GPB was submitted to PPDO and forwarded to DILG; follow-ups were made. Some GAD activities were implemented while five activities remained unimplemented; training on HGDG was planned.
observations.reason: GAD structure and trainings were implemented and the GPB submitted for review, but the DILG Certificate of Endorsement was not received. Some GAD activities remained unimplemented and the HGDG tool was not yet administered due to limited training participation.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) to: (a) document hiring of services of JO personnel through an individual contract of service, memorandum of agreement or job order indicating duties, responsibilities and work to be done during the period together with required stipulations as per CSC MC No. 17 s. 2002; (b) in coordination with department heads assess existing organizational structure so that the ideal number of plantilla personnel will be the required manpower to avoid undue number of job orders.
observations.managementAction: Management explained that starting January 2019, pro-forma forms necessary for proper documentation of hiring JO personnel were being used by the Municipality. The HRMO indicated that need for JO personnel was due to numerous programs, projects and activities implemented/supported by the Municipality.
observations.reason: Beginning January 2019 the Municipality adopted pro-forma forms to document hiring of JO personnel. However, during CY 2018 many JO personnel were engaged without formal contracts; the Municipality asserts need due to workload but documentation was improved subsequently.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to: (a) in coordination with HRMO regularly reconcile employees’ records at GSIS, PhilHealth and PAG-IBIG to identify affected employees on unremitted balances and immediately remit amounts due to these agencies; (b) identify causes of over remittance and make proper adjustments; (c) remit due GSIS loan amortizations pursuant to Section 14.2, Rule III of the IRR of R.A. No. 8291 to avoid penalties.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant stated that differences were due to leave without pay of some employees and timing/posting issues; the Municipality will reconcile with GSIS and other agencies. The Municipality maintained that they remit premiums and loans promptly and will conduct reconciliations in CY 2019.
observations.reason: Differences attributed to timing of posting, employee movements, leave without pay and pro-rated remittances for transferred employees. Municipality plans to reconcile and adjust with GSIS and other agencies; no imposed penalties reported to date.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive, as chairperson of the MCPC: (a) instruct Municipal Budget Officer to allocate 1% share from IRA for LCPC as provided in DILG MC No. 2012-120; (b) direct members of the Council to prepare the Local Development Plan for Children (LDPC) and Local Investment Plan for Children (LIPC) and maximize utilization of allocated LCPC Fund to identified PPAs; and (c) require Municipal Social Welfare Officer to ensure identified PPAs are carried out and continuously monitor implementation.
observations.managementAction: Management justified that although they did not specifically allocate 1% of IRA for LCPC, the Municipality appropriated budget and implemented various programs amounting to ₱1,670,640.00 that can be attributed for the welfare and protection of children. For CY 2019, the Municipality has allocated and included in the Appropriation Ordinance various programs, projects and activities geared towards the protection of children.
observations.reason: Management claims programs and activities benefitting children totaling ₱1,670,640.00 were implemented though not specifically coded as 1% IRA for LCPC. For CY 2019 allocations to LCPC-related programs were included; LDPC and LIPC were not prepared in CY 2018.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Office of the Human Resource Management and the Municipal Treasurer’s Office to discontinue the practice of allowing deductions on monthly loan amortizations of government/private lending institutions and capital contribution to employees’ association from the personnel’s payroll other than those mandated by law. Strictly comply with the GAA provision that monthly take-home pay of employee shall not be lower than Five Thousand Pesos (₱5,000.00).
observations.managementAction: Management requested reconsideration noting that salary deductions for loans were advantageous to employees to avoid delays and penalties and that loans from employee association and cooperatives were beneficial due to minimal charges and interest. Management commented most employees have salaries where their net take-home pay after mandatory deductions remain above ₱5,000.00. Starting CY 2019, the Municipality will review deductions to comply with GAA.
observations.reason: Management argued that deductions provide convenience and benefit employees; however, some employees’ net take-home pay remained below ₱5,000.00 in CY 2018 due to multiple authorized deductions. Municipality committed to review and adjust practices in CY 2019.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure prompt action and settlement of outstanding audit suspensions, disallowances and charges and coordinate with COA to resolve and settle the notices to avoid carrying unsettled liabilities and to improve internal control systems to prevent recurrence.
observations.managementAction: The Municipality updated records and prepared responses and supporting documents for submission to COA and will pursue settlements and negotiations as necessary. Pending Notices were acted upon and some settlements were made during the year.
observations.reason: Some notices were settled during the year (₱5,103.00) while the majority remained outstanding; Municipality continues to coordinate with COA and prepare documentation for settlement.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:
 designated GSO to dig deep additional information to detect the
unidentified, missing and stolen PPEs so as to determine the municipal
officials/employees accountable/responsible based on the Acknowledgement Receipt for
Equipment (ARE) issued to them. After the accountability has been determined, make the
latter liable for the replacement and payment of the lost items.
 Concerned municipal officials to execute deeds of donation or prepare Invoice
and Receipt of Property for the building/structures constructed and other PPEs
purchased by the municipality, respectively, to formally transfer ownership
and recording of these items to the barangay.
 Municipal Accountant to facilitate preparation of Journal Entry Voucher for
the cost of transferred property and equipment duly supported with documents
mentioned above;

We therefore recommended that the Municipal Accountant coordinate
with the Provincial Accountant for possible reconciliation of their records so
that necessary adjustments can be affected for the account Due to LGU
(Province) to fairly present inter-agency payable account in the Municipality’s
financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the Municipal
Accountant to:
 analyze and review the details of the difference of the Loans Payable
and prepare the necessary adjusting entries on the discrepancies
noted. Also, conduct another confirmation to the DBP office with
respect to the loans payable and include interest incurred and GRT
paid by the Agency;
 prepare Subsidiary Ledger for all the accounts related to the loan;
 check if there is lacking in the loan release;
 prepare the complete documentation of the payments on loan; and
 disclose in the Notes to Financial Statements the current portion of
the loan.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive thru the Local
Development Council exert efforts to fully attain the purpose and intent in
establishing the 20% Development Fund at the soonest possible time through the
following:
a. maximize appropriations of the 20% development fund released
allotment;
b. coordinate with the National Agencies, Barangay or Province which
may have projects for implementation that might coincide with the
planned project of the Municipality;
c. have proper coordination among the project beneficiaries and the
officials involved in planning, execution and monitoring of the
projects;
d. Clear identification and description of specific projects to be
implemented; and
e. Having consistency of projects in the AIP and in the budget.

We also recommended that Management cause the immediate
implementation of the projects that had been allocated funds in order to fully
attain the purpose and intent for establishing the fund at the soonest possible
time, thereby, maximizing the real value of the money on hand.

Furthermore, we recommended providing written justification for the
unimplemented projects, project implemented which are not allowed to be
charged in the 20% Development Fund and for projects with deficiencies in CAF
or Certificate of Appropriation. We also recommended the Municipality to
refrain from charging projects in the 20% Development Fund that are not
allowed on the JMC 2017-1 and those projects not in the AIP and not in the
budget.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the MDRRMO to:
 carefully plan disaster preparedness and mitigation programs and
activities with emphasis on assessment of the conditions of barangays
and other parts of the territorial area of the Municipality to identify high
disaster risk areas, identification of specific and actual projects needed
to avoid implementation of projects not in the plan and emphasis also on
the total budget available. Ensure implementation thereof in order to
maximize the LDRRM Fund. Strengthen the rescue and recovery
facilities, manpower and equipment to ensure readiness in case of
urgency; and
 update annually the list of projects and activities to be funded from
LDRRMF specially on the unexpended QRF and DRRM – MOOE of the
previous years transferred to Special Trust Fund.
We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor provide the necessary
and complete personnel under the LDRRMO so that the LDRRM functions
can be effectively and efficiently managed.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Local School Board
officials to conduct the proper planning to address the immediate specific needs of
every school especially for the lack of rooms where classes would be conducted and
other necessities of schools in accordance with the revised Joint Circular for
utilization of SEF with emphasis on the total available budget. We also recommend
to prioritize and implement projects and activities chargeable against Special
Education Fund to its optimal level to address the needs of the school children for
quality education as provided for under Section 272 of R.A. 7160 and Joint Circular
No. 1 s. 1998 of DECS, DBM and DILG.

Likewise, ensure that all expenditures are charged to sufficient allotments,
either through realigned or augmented budget. Pass resolution/ordinance
authorizing the realignment of the savings from one expense item to another within
the same expense class to avoid disallowance in audit.

Also, submit explanation/justification why some expenses were not aligned
with the program/activity/project budgeted and expended.

Further, require the Local School Board to submit justification why the
amount of ₱633,565.00 was charged against the SEF in spite provisions of the above
Joint Circular.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal
Accountant to:
 ensure that cash advances for seminars and other expenses are liquidated
within the prescribed number of days after its purpose has been served.
For failure of the accountable officers to settle/liquidate outstanding
accountabilities as soon as the purpose for which it was given has been
served, send demand letters enjoining the officials and employees
concerned. If demands were unheeded, suspension of payment of salaries
until the outstanding balance of accountabilities are fully settled shall be
enforced as provided in COA Circular No. 2012-004;
 refrain from processing disbursement vouchers for a new cash advance of
accountable officers until their previous cash advance has been settled.
Exercise utmost diligence in monitoring liquidation of cash advances; and
 ensure that cash advances are granted to the actual payees.

We recommended that the Executive Committee and the Technical Working Group:
 adhere to the complete structure and positions of the LGU GFPS provided
for in the MCW IRR such that there will be specific person assigned for
President of Person with Disabilities Organization as member of Executive
Committee, Chair on Sanggunian Committee on Appropriation will be
included in the Executive Committee and there will be officially assigned
TWG Chair and TWG Secretary;
 conduct capacity building of all members in GAD sensitivity training (GST),
gender-responsive planning and budgeting, gender analysis and gender audit
 facilitate the establishment of the GAD Database to aid in gender analysis
and provide baseline data on the status of women and men in the LGU.
Assistance of the DILG Operation Field Officer can be asked and even
barangays;
 conduct the early planning and budgeting for GAD so that the plan and
budget can be forwarded to DILG Regional Office for review and approval.
Thus, DILG- approved plan and budget can be integrated in the AIP and
budget as well. Also, consultation and meaningful participation of employees
and constituencies through accredited women or people/civil society
organizations, foundations, interfaith organizations, among others to ensure
the relevance of their GPBs can be done and LGU situation could be
analysed as well to determine gender gaps/issues and the capacity of the LGU
to respect, promote or fulfill women’s human rights or to plan, implement
and monitor GAD interventions;
 conduct meetings to monitor the implementation of GAD activities; and
 assess the extent of compliance through a compliance checklist of the
elements of the institutional mechanisms in place and intensify efforts on
those areas that should be in place.

We also recommended the members of the GFPS to provide incentive to
recognize outstanding GAD PPAs for individuals who have exemplary contributions
to GAD.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Engineer
and the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator to ensure posting of
public notice on all infrastructure projects to promote public awareness on the use
of government funds.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the General Services
Officer-Designate to meticulously and judiciously plan procurement for each
quarter as consolidated and incorporate them in the Annual Procurement Plan
(APP) in order to adopt competitive/public bidding to provide reasonable assurance
that the best quality at the lowest possible price is availed. Ensure that only those
that are emergency in nature and those ordinary or regular supplies and materials
not available in the Procurement Services (PS) and within the threshold are
purchased through shopping.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal
Accountant to prepare and immediately submit the required reports and to strictly
adhere with the rules on disclosing information in the notes to financial statements
in compliance with PPSAS.

We recommended that the Honorable Mayor require the GSO/Municipal Treasurer to maintain property cards, complete with relevant information for all PPE to facilitate reconciliation with accounting records.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant and the General Service Officer/Municipal Treasurer conducted the reconciliation of the results of the inventory count with the property and accounting records. There were items which were unidentified, missing and unserviceable. The Municipality is still with request for inspection of the said properties for disposal submitted to COA office. They asked for ample time to determine the PPEs and review and enhance their process since this finding refers to the detection of unidentified and missing items. They also submitted photocopies of documents regarding request for relief from accountability for the stolen items and schedule of unserviceable items.
observations.reason: Unidentified and missing items detection was not an easy task. That is why they are asking for ample time to determine these PPEs.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Treasurer to enforce the tax remedies provided for under Book II of the Local Government Code in the collection of taxes due to the government.
observations.managementAction: The Acting Municipal Treasurer reminded the tax payers of their obligation during the Social Pension Pay Out. This resulted to a decrease in tax delinquency from 10,395,642.91 to 5,737,468.31 as of December 31, 2017. They are planning to schedule the 20 barangays for the information dissemination relative to payment Real Property Tax.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that: a. Management adhere to the provision of Section 20, Volume I of NGAS to ensure the reliability of the balances of Real Property Tax Receivables; b. the Municipal Accountant to correct receivables set up in prior years so that the recorded amount of receivables maybe presented at a more realistic amount; c. the Municipal Accountant to maintain subsidiary ledgers for current and prior years account to have a detailed record of the accounts for facility of verification.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Treasurer reconciled the RPT receivables at the beginning of CY 2017. The variance is due to the adjustments made by the Municipal Assessor. The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer reconciled the RPT receivables at the beginning of CY 2017. The Municipal Accountant did not maintain subsidiary ledgers for the current and prior years account. The maintaining and updating of the PPE Ledgers will be started next year 2018. The Municipal Accountant indicated that maintenance of subsidiary ledgers for the current and prior years account will be possible once they have a detailed record of the accounts for verification and that they will reconcile with their records.
observations.reason: Variance is due to adjustments made by the Municipal Assessor. Subsidiary ledgers for the current and prior years account were not maintained and will be prepared once detailed records are available.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the GAD Focal Point Person to submit GAD Plan and Budget to the DILG Regional Office for review and approval and to set-up a GAD database to spearhead gender-responsive planning, programming and policy formulation and to include only eligible expenses in their GPB and Accomplishment Report.
observations.managementAction: The GAD Plan and Budget were submitted to Provincial Planning and Development Office on March 31, 2017 for review and forwarded to DILG Provincial Office on April 20, 2017. After comments they resubmitted on June 14, 2017. The management will facilitate the submission of GAD Plan and Budget and the establishment of GAD database. They also indicated that the GAD Focal Point Persons will be strengthened and conduct capacity building.
observations.reason: GAD Plan for CY 2016 was submitted late and not DILG approved. GAD database not yet established; no consultations were conducted for 2017.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a. provide budget and create a LDRRMO officer position and enforce the mandatory budgetary requirement through an appropriate ordinance as required under the above-cited regulation; b. enforce the implementation of the planned disaster activities and projects as incorporated in the LDRRMFIP; c. ensure that procurements/events are in line with the LDRRMFIP.
observations.managementAction: LDRRM officer position is created last May 22, 2017 and appointed Mr. Marcial Cerezo as MDRRMO last July 18,2017. The management coordinated with barangay captains and conducted various procurements and participated in preparedness trainings. Some procurements and donations (pick up and two second hand vehicles) were made for barangays. There were still lapses noted in implementation of disaster activities for 2017.
observations.reason: Position created and some activities implemented but lapses remained; some procurements/events were not fully aligned or implemented as planned.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: a) coordinate with the barangay officials on the implementation of the five basic environmental laws as required under RA No. 9003; b) request the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board to prioritize the finalization of the ten-year SWMP for the early identification of issues/gaps; c) initiate information drive and awareness campaign on solid waste management regulations in coordination with barangay officials.
observations.managementAction: As part of the Solid Waste Management Programs, the LGU started programs using recyclable materials and encouraged barangays to participate. The 10 year Solid Waste Management Plan of the Municipality was approved by the Solid Waste Management Commission last August 30, 2017. The LGU conducted information campaigns and various activities promoting recycling and solid waste management.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management directly coordinate with DSWD to fast track the release of fund and cycle menu upon signing of the Memorandum of Agreement and for the smooth implementation of the program; ensure that procurement plan is in place and actually followed.
observations.managementAction: The management coordinated closely with the partner agency (DSWD) to fast track the release of funds and cycle menu upon signing of the Memorandum of Agreement and for the smooth implementation of the program. They indicated the LGU will follow procurement plans and coordinate with DSWD.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Local School Board to submit explanation on why the amount P227,271.00 will not be subjected to disallowance in spite previous audit recommendation and to ensure only allowable expenses are charged to SEF.
observations.managementAction: The management replied that the remaining projects in the Local School Board Budget were not implemented due to the insufficiency of the appropriation based on the approved program estimate. They realigned unimplemented projects and appropriations to capital outlay projects and will follow up the repairs and maintenance projects. They further requested consideration for certain transactions (Scouting Activities) which were made before release of JMC No.1 s.2017; they will comply with audit recommendations.
observations.reason: Remaining projects not implemented due to insufficiency of appropriation; some transactions were made before issuance of applicable Joint Memorandum Circular.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management direct the BAC and TWG to: - Validate the contractor’s list that all possible on-going projects/awarded not yet started are included therein; - Scrutinize the contractor’s manpower and equipment on all projects to be awarded and not on an individual project only; - For ongoing projects, require the contractor to submit the name of the proposed new Project Engineer and Foreman and their qualifications.
observations.managementAction: The Bids and Awards Committee ensures that contractors with awarded contracts for CY 2017 have enough NFCC. Unlike in 2016 wherein they had one contractor for various projects, in 2017 they have improved. For ongoing projects, they requested contractors to submit name of Project Engineer and Foreman together with qualifications in order to validate capability.
observations.reason: BAC improved procedures and ensured contractors have adequate NFCC; contractors were required to submit proposed Project Engineer and Foreman for ongoing projects.
